title: @peers consensus agent: weighted expertise scoring > simple majority voting
description: Multi-LLM consensus systems should weight each model's vote by its domain expertise, not treat all models as equal. A model with 85% confidence but 0.95 architecture expertise outweighs one with 95% confidence but 0.80 architecture expertise.
root_cause: Naive multi-agent consensus treats all votes equally, ignoring that LLMs have different strengths. Claude excels at architecture, GPT-4 at implementation, Gemini at data analysis. Equal weighting dilutes expert opinions.
solution: Implement weighted consensus: Weighted Vote = Confidence % × Domain Weighting Factor. Output results in a table showing per-agent findings, votes, weights, and weighted scores. Surface dissenting views — disagreement is signal, not noise.
lesson_summary: When building multi-LLM consensus systems, assign domain-specific expertise weights to each model. Use weighted scoring (confidence × weight) rather than simple majority. Always surface valuable disagreements.

code_pattern.output: Results table: Agent | Finding | Confidence % | Weight | Weighted Score
code_pattern.consensus: Winner = highest weighted_vote sum across peers

references: globalcache/l-agence.org/INDEX.md - MIT Peers consensus research (arxiv papers)
